1: Zophar’s speech of retribution theology is the Old Testament’s most systematic statement of the principle that Christ bore in order to redeem those it condemned. The entire speech functions as a description of divine curse upon wickedness — a curse that Paul says Christ absorbed: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” (Galatians 3:13). Zophar’s theology is not entirely wrong; it just failed to account for the substitutionary resolution.
2: “Therefore my thoughts push me to answer; I feel urgency within me.” The urgency to defend divine justice is a legitimate theological impulse. Christ came to “fulfill” the law and the prophets, not abolish them (Matthew 5:17) — that is, to enact divine justice in full, not to dismiss it. The urgency Zophar feels to defend divine retributive justice is the urgency that the cross satisfies: God is just and the one who justifies (Romans 3:26).
3: Zophar’s claim to possess special understanding beyond ordinary human grasp anticipates Paul’s description of the mystery of Christ hidden and then revealed: “the mystery hidden for ages” (Colossians 1:26–27) was beyond the grasp of even the wisest wisdom teachers until the Spirit revealed it. What Zophar “understands” is the retribution pattern; what was hidden was the substitutionary resolution of that pattern in Christ.
4: “From of old, since man was set upon the earth” — Zophar appeals to the primordial order. The primordial order includes the curse on sin from Gen 3; the creation order does embed a retributive pattern. But Paul argues from the same primordial order to show that God had planned a resolution from before creation: “chosen in him before the creation of the world” (Ephesians 1:4). The ancient pattern of retribution was always destined to be resolved by the ancient plan of redemption.
5: “The triumph of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless lasts but a moment.” Applied to Christ: the apparent triumph of those who crucified him was indeed short. By Sunday morning the triumphant parties — the religious leaders who had gained their verdict, Pilate who had maintained order, death itself — found their triumph overturned. Paul in 1 Corinthians 15:55: “where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?” The triumph lasted three days; the victory of resurrection is eternal.
6: “Though his greatness mounts to the heavens and his head reaches the clouds.” The hubristic overreach into the divine sphere finds its antithesis in the incarnation: Christ descended from heaven rather than ascending to it (John 3:13: “no one has ever gone into heaven except the one who came from heaven — the Son of Man”). The one who had genuine divine height chose genuine human humility (Philippians 2:6–8), reversing the pride-pattern that Zophar describes as the mark of the doomed wicked.
7: “He will perish forever like his own dung; those who saw him will say, ‘Where is he?’” The “where is he?” of the vanished wicked becomes the “where is he?” of the empty tomb. The guards said “where is he?” when they found the tomb empty; Mary said “where have you taken him?” (John 20:13,15). But the answer is the opposite of Zophar’s intended meaning: “he is not here; he has risen!” (Matthew 28:6). The “where is he” of vanishment becomes the “where is he” of resurrection-astonishment.
8: “He flies away like a dream and cannot be found.” The vanishment of the wicked anticipates the resurrection appearances where Christ appeared and disappeared at will: “while he was eating with them, he disappeared from their sight” (Luke 24:31); appearing through locked doors (John 20:19,26). He who was expected to be permanently vanished by death cannot be found in the tomb but is repeatedly found alive — the reversal of the dream-vanishment pattern.
9: “The eye that saw him will see him no more; his own place will no longer recognize him.” The disciples on the road to Emmaus were kept from recognizing Christ (Luke 24:16: “they were kept from recognizing him”). The empty tomb meant his former place no longer contained him. But then recognition came (Luke 24:31: “their eyes were opened and they recognized him”) — the place that no longer recognized the dead one now recognizes the Risen One.
10: “His children will seek favor from the poor, and his hands will give back their wealth.” The reversal — the formerly wealthy reduced to dependence on the poor — is enacted in Christ’s incarnation: the rich one became poor so that through his poverty we might become rich (2 Corinthians 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you through his poverty might become rich”). The voluntary poverty of the incarnation is the positive counterpart to Zophar’s forced poverty of the wicked.
11: “His bones are full of the sins of his youth; they will lie down with him in the dust.” Christ’s bones held no sin of his own — yet he bore ours. His bones were deliberately preserved intact at the crucifixion (John 19:36, fulfilling Psalms 34:20: “he protects all his bones, not one of them will be broken”). The sin that should have lodged in human bones was laid on him instead; he went down into the dust carrying our sins, and rose leaving them buried.
12: “Though evil is sweet in his mouth and he hides it under his tongue.” The hiding of evil contrasts with Christ who had nothing to hide: “which one of you convicts me of sin?” (John 8:46). Yet he voluntarily took on what was hidden — he bore the secret sins, the private wickedness, the hidden darkness of all humanity (Matthew 6:4,6,18: “your Father who sees what is done in secret will reward you”). He who was transparent bore the opacity of all human sin.
13: “Though he spares it and will not let it go, but keeps it lodged in his mouth.” The retention of evil that leads to death is what Christ drank to the dregs. The cup he prayed to avoid (Matthew 26:39: “let this cup pass from me”) contained the concentrated evil of human sin; he did not spit it out but drank it fully: “my Father, if this cup cannot pass unless I drink it, may your will be done” (Matthew 26:42). He consumed what others retained, so that they would not be consumed by it.
14: “Yet his food turns in his stomach; it becomes cobra venom inside him.” The transformation of sweetness into poison recalls the bronze serpent in the wilderness: Numbers 21:6–9, where serpent venom was the judgment on sin. Christ explicitly connects himself to this scene: “just as Moses lifted up the snake in the desert, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, that everyone who believes in him may have eternal life” (John 3:14–15). He absorbed the serpent’s poison on the cross so that those who look to him are healed.
15: “He swallowed wealth down, and will vomit it up again; God will expel it from his belly.” The divine expulsion of unjust wealth points to the resurrection as the expulsion of death’s claim: death swallowed Christ (1 Corinthians 15:54: “death has been swallowed up in victory”) and then could not keep him. Hosea 13:14 (quoted in 1 Corinthians 15:55): “I will redeem them from death. Where, O death, are your plagues? Where, O grave, is your destruction?” Death swallowed him and was forced to give him up.
16: “He will suck the venom of cobras; the tongue of the viper will kill him.” The serpent’s venom as divine judgment connects to the Edenic curse (Genesis 3:15: “he will crush your head, and you will strike his heel”). Christ bore the serpent’s strike — the viper’s tongue that kills. But the crushing of the serpent’s head is the resurrection defeat of Satan: Hebrews 2:14 (“by his death he might destroy him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil”). He was struck by the serpent so that the serpent would be crushed.
17: “He will not look on the rivers, the streams, the brooks flowing with honey and butter.” The wicked man who will not taste the promised abundance contrasts with Christ who both is and provides living water: “whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst again” (John 4:14); “rivers of living water will flow from within them” (John 7:38). He was thirsty on the cross (John 19:28) so that all who come to him would never thirst.
18: “He must restore the fruits of his labor without consuming them.” Christ’s labor was entirely for others: he came “not to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Matthew 20:28). The fruits of his labor — justification, sanctification, glorification — are all given away: “freely you have received; freely give” (Matthew 10:8). He labored and others eat; he planted and others taste the fruit (John 4:37–38: “others have done the hard work, and you have reaped the benefits”).
19: “For he oppressed and abandoned the poor; he seized a house he did not build.” Christ did the opposite: he served the poor (Luke 4:18: “the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor”), championed the abandoned (the widow, the orphan, the outcast), and built a house not for himself but for others (“I am going there to prepare a place for you,” John 14:2). His entire ministry is the reversal of every social injustice Zophar catalogues.
20: “He will not feel peace within; he will retain nothing of what he desired.” Christ is the source of peace precisely because he himself entered the absence of peace in Gethsemane and at the cross: John 14:27 (“peace I leave with you; my peace I give you. I do not give to you as the world gives”). His peace in resurrection (“peace be with you,” John 20:19,21,26) comes after passing through the storm of divine judgment — peace given out of a profound personal experience of its absence.
21: “None of his food is left; therefore no one will inherit his property.” The wicked man’s total consumption leaving nothing for inheritance contrasts with Christ whose inheritance multiplies: Psalms 2:8 (“ask me, and I will make the nations your inheritance, the ends of the earth your possession”); Romans 8:17 (believers are “heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ”). He died leaving not desolation but an inheritance inexhaustible for all who receive it.
22: “In the very fullness of his plenty he will be in distress.” The reversal of abundance into distress anticipates Christ’s voluntary entry into distress: Philippians 4:11–13 (“I have learned to be content whatever the circumstances” — the opposite of this “distress in fullness”). Christ, who was the fullness of divinity (Colossians 2:9: “in Christ all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form”), entered the distress of the cross without being destroyed by it.
23: “When he is set to fill his belly, God will hurl his fierce wrath upon him.” The divine wrath poured out at the moment of intended satisfaction is the theology of the cross: Christ at his “hour” — the moment appointed for his glory — received the cup of divine wrath. The Last Supper of bread and wine was immediately followed by Gethsemane and the cross. He was set to fill others with his body and blood; what fell on him was the fierce wrath that would otherwise have fallen on them.
24: “He flees from the iron weapon, and the bronze bow strikes him through.” The inescapability of divine judgment — fleeing one weapon only to be struck by another — is the inescapability that Christ voluntarily embraced. He did not flee: “do you think I cannot call on my Father, and he will at once put at my disposal more than twelve legions of angels? But how then would the Scriptures be fulfilled?” (Matthew 26:53–54). He stood still, let the weapons fall, and thereby absorbed the inescapable judgment so others could escape.
25: “The arrow is drawn; it passes through his body; the shining blade comes out through his gall; terrors overwhelm him.” The physical description of being struck through anticipates the soldier’s lance thrust into Christ’s side (John 19:34: “one of the soldiers pierced Jesus’ side with a spear, bringing a sudden flow of blood and water”). The blade that came through Christ’s body confirmed his death — and the blood and water that flowed became the source of sacramental life for the church.
26: “All darkness is kept in store for his treasures; a fire not fanned by wind shall consume him.” The stored-up darkness — darkness associated with divine judgment — fell on Christ at the cross: “from noon until three in the afternoon darkness came over all the land” (Matthew 27:45). The spontaneous fire of divine judgment consuming the wicked becomes the fire of divine love consuming the sin that Christ bore. He emerged from the darkness in resurrection, and now light, not darkness, is kept in store for his treasures.
27: “The heavens will expose his guilt, and the earth will rise up against him.” Heaven and earth as legal witnesses against the condemned: at the cross, the opposite occurred for the convicted but innocent Christ. The centurion who watched said “surely this man was the Son of God!” (Mark 15:39) — heaven witnessed on his behalf. The earth shook (Matthew 27:51: “the earth shook, the rocks split”) — not in condemnation but in cosmic response to the death of the Creator. Heaven and earth testified to his identity, not his guilt.
28: “The produce of his house will depart; his possessions will pour away in the day of his anger.” On the day of Christ’s death, the temple curtain was torn from top to bottom (Matthew 27:51) — the possessions of the old sacrificial system poured away. But this was not loss; it was fulfillment: the “house” of the old covenant gave way to the better house of his body and the Spirit’s temple (1 Corinthians 3:16; Job 6:19; John 2:21: “the temple he had spoken of was his body”).
29: “This is the portion of the wicked man from God — the heritage decreed for him by God.” The divinely decreed heritage of wicked men is what Christ voluntarily received in their place. Galatians 3:13: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: ‘Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole.’” He accepted the heritage decreed for the wicked so that the wicked might receive the heritage of the righteous — “an inheritance that can never perish, spoil or fade” (1 Peter 1:4).
